original_abstract: During the nineteenth century, women missionaries sought and found a degree of public acceptance and professional opportunity that remained largely inaccessible to them in their home countries. While they spearheaded movements to Christianize and modernize Asian and African societies through evangelism, education, and healthcare, their methods and motivations have since become subjects of critical inquiry. This article argues for a rediscovery of the sacrificial dedication that defined these women, positioning them as central figures in what became the most significant era of Christian expansion. These were remarkable individuals who abandoned domestic security to labor abroad, often leaving an indelible mark on the communities where they settled. By prioritizing eternal spiritual values over worldly gain, they serve as enduring examples for the present generation. Ultimately, their lives reflected the Pauline conviction of pressing toward a higher calling, demonstrating a profound commitment to settling "eternal values" at the expense of temporal comforts.
humanized_abstract: Women missionaries in the 19th century received more public acceptance and career opportunities abroad than they did at home. As pioneers of evangelical and social reforms through evangelism, education and medicine in various parts of Asia and Africa, their evangelising methods and motivations have been the subject of increasing critique. This article calls for a re-appraisal of these women missionaries and their self-sacrificing devotion to their work, acknowledging their role as catalysts of what is arguably the most significant period of Christian expansion in history. These women who crossed the comfort of their homes to toil in foreign lands and make a lasting impact on the lands to which they travelled, exemplified in their lives the Pauline principle of striving for higher things, investing in eternal values while eschewing earthly gain.
